bins.name: Flexibility as System Value
bins.desc: Kiesling argues that modern data centers and hyper-flexible loads such as thermal energy storage possess latent flexibility across multiple time scales that current rules ignore. She supports interconnection and market rules that recognize and reward this flexibility, citing research showing that modest curtailment commitments could integrate tens of gigawatts of new load on existing capacity.
quotes: Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.

Norris et al. (2025) quantifies this potential: it finds that, with modest curtailment of just 0.25 %–0.5 % of annual uptime, U.S. balancing authorities could integrate nearly 76 GW–98 GW of new large loads using existing capacity without disruptive grid upgrades.
Hyper flexible loads like TES are being installed now with the operational capability and business model incentive to make robust, enforceable and dependable flexibility commitments.
The expedited queue access proposal is one step in the direction of valuing flexibility as a capability.
Aligning cost allocation with marginal impacts ensures that flexible loads remain part of the bulk system rather than retreating into private microgrids.

bins.name: Marginal-Cost Allocation Reform
bins.desc: Kiesling opposes both embedded-cost and fully distributed cost methodologies and the ANOPR's 100% participant funding proposal. She argues these methods are economically unsound because they ignore marginal impacts, produce arbitrary results based on queue position, and push elastic large loads toward grid defection. She recommends replacing them with Ramsey-Boiteux inverse-elasticity pricing.
quotes: Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.
The ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades relies on flawed embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal impacts.
Efficient cost recovery should instead follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles, allocating fixed costs inversely to elasticity.
Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.

Transmission upgrade cost assignment remains one of the least transparent components of the interconnection process. Because most jurisdictions rely on embedded-cost or FDC methods, the relationship between a project's marginal impact and its assigned upgrade cost is weak.
FERC should not adopt a blanket rule obligating large loads to bear 100 percent of assigned upgrade costs without first reforming the allocation methodology to reflect marginal cost principles.
Aligning cost allocation with marginal impacts ensures that flexible loads remain part of the bulk system rather than retreating into private microgrids.

bins.name: Study Process Modernization
bins.desc: Kiesling supports modernizing interconnection studies to model actual flexible operational profiles rather than peak nameplate demand. She cautions against mechanically importing the generator interconnection process to loads, as it was designed for resources that inject power and embeds outdated assumptions about load rigidity.
quotes: The rapid growth of AI data center load presents a critical coordination challenge due to the temporal mismatch between rapid data center deployment (18–24 months) and slow grid infrastructure expansion (7–10 years).

Norris et al. (2025) quantifies this potential: it finds that, with modest curtailment of just 0.25 %–0.5 % of annual uptime, U.S. balancing authorities could integrate nearly 76 GW–98 GW of new large loads using existing capacity without disruptive grid upgrades.

Applying a generator-style interconnection regime to large loads risks entrenching these problems further. The generator process is designed for resources that inject power, not for large, flexible loads whose operational behaviors differ fundamentally from generators.

bins.name: Co-location as Rational Response
bins.desc: Kiesling frames co-location and hybrid configurations as rational institutional responses to high transaction costs and the pacing mismatch, not as bypass strategies to penalize. She argues that extending generator-style rules to loads without modification would worsen rather than resolve these frictions.
quotes: Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.
The hybrid or co-location contracts that large loads and generators are entering are system-enhancing examples of the 'make or buy' decision in institutional economics.
Co-location is a market response to the pacing mismatch between data center speed-to-power and infrastructure construction timeframes.

bins.name: Keep Loads on the Bulk System
bins.desc: Kiesling argues that poor cost allocation drives flexible loads into private microgrids, undermining reliability. Marginal-cost-aligned tariffs are necessary to keep grid-supportive large loads interconnected rather than isolated.
quotes: Aligning cost allocation with marginal impacts ensures that flexible loads remain part of the bulk system rather than retreating into private microgrids.

bins.name: Oppose Blanket 100% Participant Funding
bins.desc: Kiesling directly opposes the ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades, arguing it rests on methodologies that do not reflect marginal cost causation and that assigning full lumpy upgrade costs to elastic loads maximizes distortion and bypass incentives.
quotes: The ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades relies on flawed embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal impacts.
Efficient cost recovery should instead follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles, allocating fixed costs inversely to elasticity.
Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.

Transmission upgrade cost assignment remains one of the least transparent components of the interconnection process. Because most jurisdictions rely on embedded-cost or FDC methods, the relationship between a project's marginal impact and its assigned upgrade cost is weak.
FERC should not adopt a blanket rule obligating large loads to bear 100 percent of assigned upgrade costs without first reforming the allocation methodology to reflect marginal cost principles.

bins.name: Support Expedited Access with Caveats
bins.desc: Kiesling views the expedited queue access proposal for flexible loads as a positive step but insufficient without broader flexibility recognition in interconnection studies and cost allocation.
quotes: The expedited queue access proposal is one step in the direction of valuing flexibility as a capability.

bins.name: Clarify Jurisdictional Treatment
bins.desc: Kiesling identifies inconsistent jurisdictional treatment of TES and hybrid resources as a transaction cost barrier and recommends that FERC clarify that wholesale electricity purchases for conversion to thermal energy qualify as wholesale transactions.
quotes: Applying a generator-style interconnection regime to large loads risks entrenching these problems further. The generator process is designed for resources that inject power, not for large, flexible loads whose operational behaviors differ fundamentally from generators.

A TES resource that charges with electricity and discharges thermal energy to an industrial host becomes simultaneously a wholesale and retail customer, even though its grid-facing behavior is indistinguishable from a purely electrical TES asset.

bins.name: Facilitate Hybrid and Co-location Rights
bins.desc: Kiesling supports facilitating hybrid configurations and co-location arrangements as legitimate institutional responses to high transaction costs, noting that ERCOT's Private Use Network rules have made Texas competitive for hybrid facilities.
quotes: The hybrid or co-location contracts that large loads and generators are entering are system-enhancing examples of the 'make or buy' decision in institutional economics.

bins.name: Grid-Datacenter Pacing Gap
bins.desc: Kiesling identifies the 18 to 24 month data center build time versus 7 to 10 year grid infrastructure timeline as the central coordination problem the ANOPR must address. This mismatch drives both co-location decisions and the urgency of flexibility-based solutions.
quotes: The rapid growth of AI data center load presents a critical coordination challenge due to the temporal mismatch between rapid data center deployment (18–24 months) and slow grid infrastructure expansion (7–10 years).
Co-location is a market response to the pacing mismatch between data center speed-to-power and infrastructure construction timeframes.

bins.name: TES Market Participation Barriers
bins.desc: Kiesling uses thermal energy storage as a case study in how inconsistent jurisdictional classification raises transaction costs and blocks market access for a technology with strong flexibility potential. Three configurations of TES that impose similar net loads receive divergent regulatory treatment.
quotes: Hyper flexible loads like TES are being installed now with the operational capability and business model incentive to make robust, enforceable and dependable flexibility commitments.

A TES resource that charges with electricity and discharges thermal energy to an industrial host becomes simultaneously a wholesale and retail customer, even though its grid-facing behavior is indistinguishable from a purely electrical TES asset.

bins.name: Upgrade Cost Transparency
bins.desc: Kiesling identifies upgrade cost assignment as the least transparent part of the interconnection process, producing outcomes that vary by orders of magnitude across similarly sized projects due to queue position rather than actual marginal impact.
quotes: Transmission upgrade cost assignment remains one of the least transparent components of the interconnection process. Because most jurisdictions rely on embedded-cost or FDC methods, the relationship between a project's marginal impact and its assigned upgrade cost is weak.
